content.description:
# Hob View Conversion Tracker

Tracking progress of converting JS views to Hob on the `hob-views` branch.

## North Star

Hob should be the obvious choice for *any* user-programming within Hobson. There should be no reason to choose JS except for familiarity. Hob — and its integration with Hobson (item references, code-as-data, structural editing, queryable AST) — should be better than JS for most things, and as good for the rest.

This reframes the remaining work. The goal is not "add enough primitives to convert the remaining views" — it's to make Hob genuinely superior for Hobson programming. Every primitive should be designed so that Hob is what you'd choose even if you knew JS perfectly.

## Converted (15/41)

- sortable-list-view
- note-widget-view
- widget-markdown-view
- event-definition-view
- item-search-view
- error-view
- inline-card-view
- compact-card-view
- related-items-view
- element-inspector-view
- tag-browser-view
- error-list-view
- symbol-browser-view
- widget-slider-view *(first view using pageContext via invoke)*
- widget-radio-group-view

## Deferred — Needs Hob Primitives

All remaining unconverted views need one or more capabilities Hob doesn't have yet. Conversion is paused until the needed primitives are built.

| View | Lines | Blocking primitive(s) |
|------|------:|------|
| type-workshop-view | 750 | Persistent view state (mutable state across re-renders) |
| spatial-canvas-view | 617 | Sub-item rendering, spatial primitives, persistent state |
| system-browser-view | 575 | Persistent view state (multi-pane stateful UI) |
| viewport-view | 384 | Sub-item rendering, document events, browser history API |
| app-page-view | 374 | Sub-item rendering, view composition (pageContext), design mode |
| test-suite-view | 295 | Nested instance lifecycle, inter-frame messaging |
| hobson-instance-view | 147 | iframe creation, history.pushState monkey-patching |
| modal-frame-view | 75 | Sub-item rendering, DOM lifecycle (overlay add/remove), document events |
| kernel:default-view | — | System fallback, must stay JS until Hob is fully trusted |

## Skipped — JS-native (for now)

These views exist to execute user-specified JS code (`new Function(...)`) and would gain nothing from Hob conversion today. Once Hob is the default user-programming language, inline JS execution becomes the exception rather than the norm — these views may eventually become Hob-native too.

- widget-button-view — executes inline JS on click
- script-view — displays and runs JS code items
- widget-checkbox-group-view — executes user JS to load options, unused, poor design-mode UX

## Skipped — Trivial generic-view wrappers

~12 small views (2–10 lines) that just delegate to generic-view. Not worth converting — no logic to exercise.

## Where Hob is already better than JS

- **Item references** (`@my-note`) are first-class, not string constants
- **Domain ops** (`get-field`, `set-field!`, `navigate!`) express Hobson concepts directly
- **Homoiconicity** — macros can transform code structurally
- **Sandboxed evaluation** — the system can reason about what code does

## Roadmap

### 1. JSON AST as canonical representation (foundational)

See: [JSON AST as Canonical Code Representation](c0c0c0c0-0051-0000-0000-000000000000)

This is foundational infrastructure, not a "nice to have later." It should come before or alongside the new primitives because:

- Code stored as JSON AST makes Hob code **queryable** without parsing ("find all views that call `navigate!`" is a recursive array walk, not a regex)
- **Structural editing** becomes possible, eliminating syntax errors entirely — a categorical advantage over JS
- Macros and tooling operate on the **real representation**, not a parsed-then-serialized copy
- Primitives we build will be Hob code items — better to store them in the canonical representation from the start
- Closes the last gap in "everything is an item" — code was the one holdout stored as opaque text

### 2. Persistent view state (highest-impact primitive)

Not just "atoms as a workaround for not having `let`" but named reactive state that survives re-renders, is inspectable in the inspector, and is automatically cleaned up. The system *understands* view state.

- Unlocks: type-workshop, system-browser, spatial-canvas

### 3. Sub-item rendering

Not just `api.renderItem(el, id)` with Hob syntax, but declarative composition where the system handles lifecycle. No manual mount/unmount, no orphaned DOM nodes.

- Unlocks: viewport, app-page, spatial-canvas, modal-frame

### 4. Document-level events with cleanup

Lifecycle-aware event binding that the system manages, not manual addEventListener/removeEventListener bookkeeping.

- Unlocks: viewport, modal-frame

### 5. Reactivity / dependency tracking (long game)

If Hob tracked which fields a view reads, it could auto-invalidate just that view when those fields change. JS can't do this without a framework; Hob can do it at the language level.

### 6. Structural editor

Enabled by JSON AST. The strongest UX argument for Hob over JS — edit code structure directly, no syntax errors possible, the system can assist meaningfully because it understands what you're editing.
